feedback.text: To the European Commission, I am writing to respectfully offer a suggestion for the Digital Fairness Act, a vital initiative that seeks to address problematic practices within the digital space. While the Act currently focuses on issues such as dark patterns, addictive design, and challenging subscription models, I believe there is a crucial adjacent area that warrants inclusion: the permanent destruction of video games that customers have already purchased and paid for. For many, video games are not just products; they are experiences that become an integral part of our lives, holding significant nostalgic and personal value. The ongoing issue of publishers disabling access to games, especially online-only titles without an end-of-life plan, means that these cherished digital works can vanish forever, depriving players of something they genuinely own and often deeply care about. This is not merely an inconvenience; it represents a fundamental undermining of customer ownership in the digital realm. Hundreds of games have already been destroyed, and countless more are at risk. Incorporating protections against this practice into the Digital Fairness Act would not only align with the Act's broader goals of ensuring fair digital practices but would also safeguard the digital heritage and consumer rights of millions of Europeans who value their purchased games. We hope you will consider this important addition.
